message.content: Adversaries may exploit systemd services to maintain persistence on Linux systems by creating or modifying service unit files, which control how services boot and under what conditions. Systemd, the default init system on many Linux distributions, uses these unit files to manage background processes. Malicious actors typically require root privileges to alter files in critical directories like /etc/systemd/system and /usr/lib/systemd/system, but can achieve user-level persistence by modifying files in user-specific directories such as ~/.config/systemd/user/. This technique allows adversaries to execute malicious commands at system boot, ensuring their payloads are repeatedly executed. Notable malware such as Exaramel for Linux, Fysbis, Hildegard, Pupy, Rocke, and TeamTNT have utilized systemd services for persistence. Mitigation strategies include monitoring and restricting access to systemd service directories, employing file integrity monitoring, and using least privilege principles to limit the ability of users to create or modify service unit files. Regular audits and anomaly detection can also help identify unauthorized changes to systemd services.
